title: Beverly City Council Special Meeting 06/25/2026
headline: City Council approved $1.5 million in road repairs and $45,000 for Lynch Park climate resiliency work before summer recess, while deferring action on a major National Grid underground utility project.
bullets: Road Resurfacing Transfer ($1.5M): The council approved moving $1.5 million from the road resurfacing account to fund street and sidewalk repairs across the city, with major focus on Goat Hill, Wellman Street, Weber Street, and other areas impacted by the National Grid 115 kV project. The funds come from utility company fees paid when they open streets.

Lynch Park Climate Resiliency Project ($45K): Approved a $45,000 free cash transfer for supplemental environmental permitting work needed to keep the project on track. The city is pursuing shovel-ready status for seawall protection near the Rose Garden and Carriage House; a $488,000 state grant decision is expected in July/August.

National Grid Underground Facilities (Order 182): The council approved National Grid's petition to install underground electric conduit along River, Federal, Park, Elliott, and Rantoul Streets (part of downtown reliability improvements), but with concerns about timing and coordination with ongoing construction. Council member Crowley expressed "National Grid fatigue" given multiple projects affecting Wards 2 and 3.

Conservation Commission Appointments: Approved the reappointment of Christine Bertoni and the appointment of Joanne Miller (with a waiver of the registered voter requirement) to the Conservation Commission.
Alcohol Permits for Beverly 400+ Events: Approved waivers allowing the Bubble Bar and Bottle Shop to serve alcohol on the Beverly Commons during Beverly 400+ celebrations.
votes: Conservation Commission appointment (Miller) + voter waiver — Approved 7-0 (both votes)
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary
**June 25, 2026**

## Headline
City Council approved $1.5 million in road repairs and $45,000 for Lynch Park climate resiliency work before summer recess, while deferring action on a major National Grid underground utility project.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Road Resurfacing Transfer ($1.5M)** – The council approved moving $1.5 million from the road resurfacing account to fund street and sidewalk repairs across the city, with major focus on Goat Hill, Wellman Street, Weber Street, and other areas impacted by the National Grid 115 kV project. The funds come from utility company fees paid when they open streets.

• **Lynch Park Climate Resiliency Project ($45K)** – Approved a $45,000 free cash transfer for supplemental environmental permitting work needed to keep the project on track. The city is pursuing shovel-ready status for seawall protection near the Rose Garden and Carriage House; a $488,000 state grant decision is expected in July/August.

• **National Grid Underground Facilities (Order 182)** – The council approved National Grid's petition to install underground electric conduit along River, Federal, Park, Elliott, and Rantoul Streets (part of downtown reliability improvements), but with concerns about timing and coordination with ongoing construction. Council member Crowley expressed "National Grid fatigue" given multiple projects affecting Wards 2 and 3.

• **Conservation Commission Appointments** – Approved the reappointment of Christine Bertoni and the appointment of Joanne Miller (with a waiver of the registered voter requirement) to the Conservation Commission.

• **Alcohol Permits for Beverly 400+ Events** – Approved waivers allowing the Bubble Bar and Bottle Shop to serve alcohol on the Beverly Commons during Beverly 400+ celebrations.

## Key Votes

| Order | Item | Result |
|-------|------|--------|
| 166 | Road resurfacing transfer ($1.5M) | **Approved 7-0** |
| 167 | Lynch Park MEPA permitting ($45K) | **Approved 7-0** |
| 182 | National Grid underground facilities | **Approved 7-0** |
| 173 | Conservation Commission reappointment (Bertoni) | **Approved 7-0** |
| 174 | Conservation Commission appointment (Miller) + voter waiver | **Approved 7-0** (both votes) |
| 184 | Bubble Bar alcohol waiver | **Approved 7-0** |
| 185 | Bottle Shop alcohol waiver | **Approved 7-0** |

**Note:** This was the council's final meeting before summer recess.

title: School Committee: Committee of the Whole Mtg 06/24/2026 (hybrid with captions)
headline: The committee reluctantly accepted a FY27 school budget $2M below its own request in a 5-4 vote, while also passing a new AI/deepfake policy and reviewing a strongly positive first-year evaluation of Superintendent Cushing.
bullets: FY27 budget acceptance (5-4): The committee voted to formally accept the $89,876,235 appropriation set by City Council on June 23rd — $4.5M more than the current year, but only $3,621 above the balanced budget the committee had reviewed, after the council declined to fund the committee's full "critical needs" request. Several members noted the vote was a formality since legal counsel confirmed it wasn't required, but chose to vote anyway for transparency.

AI & deepfake policy (second reading): Approved an updated policy prohibiting students, staff, volunteers, and contractors from creating or distributing deepfakes/synthetic media, with new protections for employees against AI impersonation, a requirement for annual policy review, and a directive to develop detailed AI procedures over the summer.

Superintendent evaluation: The committee reviewed (informational, no vote) Dr. Cushing's first-year performance evaluation — ratings landed mostly at "proficient" to "exemplary" across goals and standards, with a bonus goal rated "significant progress" by all evaluators.

Recognitions: Honored numerous student athletes for NEC, Salem News, and MIAA tournament achievements (including a Girls Hockey NEC Championship), and recognized 17 retiring staff members for a combined 400+ years of service to the district.

Endicott partnership: Endicott College's new Center for Civic Participation director presented plans to partner with Beverly Public Schools on civics programming, including a proposed Beverly Middle School student speaker for Endicott's 9/11 25th anniversary commemoration.

Other business: Discussed DESE attrition-rate data showing most "lost" students are enrolling in vocational programs (Essex Tech) rather than leaving the district; reported 2 of 4 union bargaining units have reached verbal agreements; held executive session to discuss BSADA litigation and AFSCME contract strategy.
votes: AI & Deepfake Policy (amended, second reading) — Approved 9-0
Enter executive session (purposes 7 & 3) — Approved by roll call 9-0
raw:
# Beverly School Committee Meeting Summary
**June 24, 2026 (Committee of the Whole)**

## Headline
The committee reluctantly accepted a FY27 school budget $2M below its own request in a 5-4 vote, while also passing a new AI/deepfake policy and reviewing a strongly positive first-year evaluation of Superintendent Cushing.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **FY27 budget acceptance (5-4)** – Accepted the $89,876,235 appropriation set by City Council on June 23rd, well below the committee's own "critical needs" request, in what several members called a formality vote taken for transparency.

• **AI & deepfake policy** – Approved (second reading, amended) a policy prohibiting deepfakes/synthetic media, with employee protections and a directive to build detailed AI procedures over the summer.

• **Superintendent evaluation** – Reviewed Dr. Cushing's first-year evaluation; ratings landed mostly proficient-to-exemplary (informational).

• **Recognitions** – Honored student athletes (including a Girls Hockey NEC Championship) and 17 retiring staff members.

• **Endicott partnership** – Discussed a new civics-education partnership with Endicott College's Center for Civic Participation.

• **Other business** – Attrition data, union bargaining update, and executive session on litigation/bargaining strategy.

## Key Votes

| Item | Result |
|------|--------|
| Accept FY27 budget appropriation ($89,876,235) | **Approved 5-4** |
| AI & Deepfake Policy (amended) | **Approved 9-0** |
| Enter executive session | **Approved 9-0 (roll call)** |

title: Beverly City Council Budget Meeting (Final Vote) 06/23/2026
headline: Beverly City Council approved the FY27 budget after voting down most proposed cuts and passing one modest amendment reducing the mayor's discretionary travel/expense budget.
bullets: **Budget Overview**: The council held its final vote on the proposed FY27 budget after a month-long review process. The budget includes reductions in city spending (10-20% in some departments) but the school budget was reduced less than the Finance Committee recommended. Councilors discussed the city's cash reserves (now projected at 17% of the budget, down from 20% a year ago) and the structural deficit expected next year.

**Mayor's Opening Statement**: Mayor Cahill emphasized that reserves have been strategically used to address city needs and cannot be depleted further due to the need to prepare for potential economic downturns. He asked the council not to cut a line item providing COLA increases (~3%) to non-union department heads and other confidential employees, noting they would receive less than unionized staff getting 3-4%+ increases.

**DEI Director Position**: After extensive debate, the council voted 5-4 to eliminate the Racial Justice/DEI Director position ($62,621). Councilors disagreed sharply—some argued the position was essential given national events and the city's history; others (including the Mayor) urged against elimination. The Mayor had proposed reducing it to 20 hours instead of cutting it entirely. Proponents noted the Human Rights Committee could continue some work.

**Travel/Discretionary Spending Amendment**: The council voted 9-0 to reduce the mayor's executive discretionary line (travel, conferences, expenses) from $13,500 to $10,000 (a $3,500 cut). Discussion revealed the line had funded beneficial items like hydrant painting by Triangle nonprofit, temporary restrooms at athletic facilities, and attendance at the Burlington sanitation conference.

**Failed Amendment Proposals**: Councilor St. Hilaire's motion to completely eliminate the travel line ($13,500 to $0) failed 3-6. His motion to cut the Constituent Services Director position ($77,625) also failed 2-7, with multiple councilors noting the role helps them serve constituents more effectively amid increasing service cuts elsewhere.

**Structural Deficit Concerns**: Councilor Houseman warned next year's budget crisis will be "much worse than this year" and proposed longer-term solutions: kindergarten fee increases, reduced health insurance contributions (currently 80% paid by city), avoiding salary increases beyond Proposition 2½ limits, and advocating for increased state aid. He cautioned that cutting entire departments (Council on Aging, Recreation, closing Beverly Farms Library) still wouldn't solve next year's $2M+ gap. He also defended the City Hall renovation project, noting $5M already spent and risks to the city's bond rating.

**Persistent Themes**: Councilor St. Hilaire, the chief advocate for deeper cuts, proposed 12 amendments totaling $724,320 in reductions, arguing spending is "unsustainable." He also urged the Mayor to cancel the $2M City Hall project. Other councilors raised concerns about transparency and resident frustration with the trash fee increase and budget process.

**(Unclear from transcript)**: Whether any other amendments besides the travel/expense reduction were voted on, or whether the full FY27 budget ultimately passed. The transcript appears truncated mid-discussion.
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary
**June 23, 2026**

## Headline
Beverly City Council approved the FY27 budget after voting down most proposed cuts and passing one modest amendment reducing the mayor's discretionary travel/expense budget.

## Key Topics & Decisions

- **Budget Overview**: The council held its final vote on the proposed FY27 budget after a month-long review process. The budget includes reductions in city spending (10-20% in some departments) but the school budget was reduced less than the Finance Committee recommended. Councilors discussed the city's cash reserves (now projected at 17% of the budget, down from 20% a year ago) and the structural deficit expected next year.

- **Mayor's Opening Statement**: Mayor Cahill emphasized that reserves have been strategically used to address city needs and cannot be depleted further due to the need to prepare for potential economic downturns. He asked the council not to cut a line item providing COLA increases (~3%) to non-union department heads and other confidential employees, noting they would receive less than unionized staff getting 3-4%+ increases.

- **DEI Director Position**: After extensive debate, the council voted 5-4 to eliminate the Racial Justice/DEI Director position ($62,621). Councilors disagreed sharply—some argued the position was essential given national events and the city's history; others (including the Mayor) urged against elimination. The Mayor had proposed reducing it to 20 hours instead of cutting it entirely. Proponents noted the Human Rights Committee could continue some work.

- **Travel/Discretionary Spending Amendment**: The council voted 9-0 to reduce the mayor's executive discretionary line (travel, conferences, expenses) from $13,500 to $10,000 (a $3,500 cut). Discussion revealed the line had funded beneficial items like hydrant painting by Triangle nonprofit, temporary restrooms at athletic facilities, and attendance at the Burlington sanitation conference.

- **Failed Amendment Proposals**: Councilor St. Hilaire's motion to completely eliminate the travel line ($13,500 to $0) failed 3-6. His motion to cut the Constituent Services Director position ($77,625) also failed 2-7, with multiple councilors noting the role helps them serve constituents more effectively amid increasing service cuts elsewhere.

- **Structural Deficit Concerns**: Councilor Houseman warned next year's budget crisis will be "much worse than this year" and proposed longer-term solutions: kindergarten fee increases, reduced health insurance contributions (currently 80% paid by city), avoiding salary increases beyond Proposition 2½ limits, and advocating for increased state aid. He cautioned that cutting entire departments (Council on Aging, Recreation, closing Beverly Farms Library) still wouldn't solve next year's $2M+ gap. He also defended the City Hall renovation project, noting $5M already spent and risks to the city's bond rating.

- **Persistent Themes**: Councilor St. Hilaire, the chief advocate for deeper cuts, proposed 12 amendments totaling $724,320 in reductions, arguing spending is "unsustainable." He also urged the Mayor to cancel the $2M City Hall project. Other councilors raised concerns about transparency and resident frustration with the trash fee increase and budget process.

- **(Unclear from transcript)**: Whether any other amendments besides the travel/expense reduction were voted on, or whether the full FY27 budget ultimately passed. The transcript appears truncated mid-discussion.

## Key Votes

| Motion | Result | Vote |
|--------|--------|------|
| Reduce mayor's discretionary travel/expense line from $13,500 to $0 | **Failed** | 3-6 |
| Reduce mayor's discretionary travel/expense line from $13,500 to $10,000 | **Passed** | 9-0 |
| Eliminate DEI Director position (line 51141) | **Passed** | 5-4 |
| Eliminate Constituent Services Director position (line 51137) | **Failed** | 2-7 |

title: Beverly City Council Budget Meeting (Public Hearing) 06/22/2026
headline: Beverly City Council held a public hearing on the proposed FY27 budget, which faces a $3.9 million deficit, with community members raising concerns about school funding, the $30 million city hall renovation, and the process for budget decision-making.
bullets: **Budget Process & Timeline**: The Finance and Property subcommittee referred the proposed FY27 budget back to the full council for a public hearing tonight. Final council discussion and voting will occur tomorrow evening. The council cannot increase budget line items but may propose cuts as amendments.

**Public Concerns on School Funding**: Multiple speakers (including educators and parents) criticized Mayor Cahill's proposed budget for underfunding Beverly Public Schools compared to neighboring communities. One teacher noted that German language instruction will be eliminated, and special education staffing is below needed levels. The School Committee had recommended a higher budget; the mayor's proposal includes a $2.25 million cut.

**Chapter 70 State Funding Disparity**: A resident highlighted that Beverly receives significantly less per-pupil state funding ($3,600) compared to Salem ($7,600)—a $4,000 difference per student costing the district over $20 million annually. Speakers blamed this on decisions dating to the 1995 closure of Beverly High School's vocational program.

**Budget Governance Structure Concerns**: The educators union president argued that concentrated executive power limits the City Council's authority over school appropriations. She urged the council to pursue charter or legal changes to give the legislative body greater oversight of school spending decisions.

**Alternative Cost-Saving Suggestions**: Residents proposed closing Beverly Farms Library (noting low usage numbers), implementing every-other-week recycling (potentially saving $500,000), negotiating health insurance rates, and requesting free teaching fellows from Endicott College rather than paying $19,100 per fellow.

**City Hall Renovation Questioned**: Multiple speakers opposed the proposed $30 million renovation of city hall, arguing the funds should prioritize schools, infrastructure, and services when the city faces a structural deficit.

**Process Transparency**: A resident asked why the community is not given earlier input during budget development rather than at the "11th hour" before voting. Council leadership explained that budget meetings occur throughout June (available on BevCam and online), with financial forecasting beginning in November/December, and encouraged earlier public engagement next year.
votes: Motion to take agenda out of order (to refer budget back to full council) — Passed 9-0
Finance and Property motion to refer proposed budget to Council of the Whole — Passed 3-0
Motion to enter executive session (collective bargaining & litigation strategy) — Passed 9-0
Motion to adjourn open meeting portion — Passed 9-0
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary
**June 22, 2026**

## Headline
Beverly City Council held a public hearing on the proposed FY27 budget, which faces a $3.9 million deficit, with community members raising concerns about school funding, the $30 million city hall renovation, and the process for budget decision-making.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Budget Process & Timeline**: The Finance and Property subcommittee referred the proposed FY27 budget back to the full council for a public hearing tonight. Final council discussion and voting will occur tomorrow evening. The council cannot increase budget line items but may propose cuts as amendments.

• **Public Concerns on School Funding**: Multiple speakers (including educators and parents) criticized Mayor Cahill's proposed budget for underfunding Beverly Public Schools compared to neighboring communities. One teacher noted that German language instruction will be eliminated, and special education staffing is below needed levels. The School Committee had recommended a higher budget; the mayor's proposal includes a $2.25 million cut.

• **Chapter 70 State Funding Disparity**: A resident highlighted that Beverly receives significantly less per-pupil state funding ($3,600) compared to Salem ($7,600)—a $4,000 difference per student costing the district over $20 million annually. Speakers blamed this on decisions dating to the 1995 closure of Beverly High School's vocational program.

• **Budget Governance Structure Concerns**: The educators union president argued that concentrated executive power limits the City Council's authority over school appropriations. She urged the council to pursue charter or legal changes to give the legislative body greater oversight of school spending decisions.

• **Alternative Cost-Saving Suggestions**: Residents proposed closing Beverly Farms Library (noting low usage numbers), implementing every-other-week recycling (potentially saving $500,000), negotiating health insurance rates, and requesting free teaching fellows from Endicott College rather than paying $19,100 per fellow.

• **City Hall Renovation Questioned**: Multiple speakers opposed the proposed $30 million renovation of city hall, arguing the funds should prioritize schools, infrastructure, and services when the city faces a structural deficit.

• **Process Transparency**: A resident asked why the community is not given earlier input during budget development rather than at the "11th hour" before voting. Council leadership explained that budget meetings occur throughout June (available on BevCam and online), with financial forecasting beginning in November/December, and encouraged earlier public engagement next year.

## Key Votes

| Vote | Result |
|------|--------|
| Motion to take agenda out of order (to refer budget back to full council) | **Passed 9-0** |
| Finance and Property motion to refer proposed budget to Council of the Whole | **Passed 3-0** |
| Motion to enter executive session (collective bargaining & litigation strategy) | **Passed 9-0** |
| Motion to adjourn open meeting portion | **Passed 9-0** |

**Note**: The final vote on the FY27 budget is scheduled for tomorrow evening and has not yet occurred.

title: Beverly City Council Budget Meeting 06/17/2026
headline: Beverly City Council approved budget presentations for the civilian dispatch center and Essex North Shore Agricultural & Technical School, with discussion focusing on staffing needs and cost efficiency.
bullets: **Dispatch Center Budget (FY27):** The civilian dispatch center presented its first full-year operating budget. Key changes include adding one full-time dispatcher (bringing total to 11 FTEs plus supervisor) to reduce overtime from the previous year's mid-year adjustment. The original 10-dispatcher model created unsustainable overtime due to loss of five part-time staff during transition.

**Equipment & Technology Upgrade:** A $46,000 equipment line item increase (253% jump) covers server warranties, aging computer replacement, and implementation of RapidSOS software—a 911 enhancement providing real-time cell phone location data, automatic language translation (80-160 languages), and emergency contact information access. This addresses past reliance on state language line services that caused delays.

**Mutual Aid Contribution:** The dispatch center receives a $10,000 annual stipend ($1,000 per full-time dispatcher) for serving as Essex County's fire control point. Some councilors questioned whether other communities should reimburse Beverly for this hub function, though this is a contractual arrangement from the 1970s.

**Essex North Shore Agricultural & Technical School Budget:** Dr. Riccio presented a regional vocational school serving 1,927 students (223 from Beverly) across 58 communities and three counties. Budget increased 4.5% through enrollment growth and grant funding rather than operational cuts. The school implemented a straight lottery admissions system in February 2025 (per state mandate) and committed to capping Beverly in-district enrollment at 427 students for three years.

**Staffing & Efficiency Concerns:** Multiple councilors pressed about potential budget cuts. Both dispatch and fire leadership emphasized that further reductions would harm response times and service quality. Chief O'Connor noted response times have improved significantly under the unified dispatch system and warned against cutting supervisor position or staffing levels.

**Language Access & Community Data:** Dispatch center confirmed Spanish as primary language need, with recent addition of third Spanish-speaking dispatcher. Council member Sonya asked about tracking language call data to inform city-wide communication strategies; dispatch indicated state language line may have logs but nothing tracked in-house.
votes: **(Unclear from transcript):** Specific salary increase percentages for Essex Tech's recently ratified teacher contract were not disclosed, pending public release.
Dispatch center offered to host council members for a facility tour to observe operations firsthand.
Essex Tech reported 41% of incoming Beverly ninth-graders have learning disabilities, suggesting strong special education support.
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary

## Headline
Beverly City Council approved budget presentations for the civilian dispatch center and Essex North Shore Agricultural & Technical School, with discussion focusing on staffing needs and cost efficiency.

## Key Topics & Decisions

- **Dispatch Center Budget (FY27):** The civilian dispatch center presented its first full-year operating budget. Key changes include adding one full-time dispatcher (bringing total to 11 FTEs plus supervisor) to reduce overtime from the previous year's mid-year adjustment. The original 10-dispatcher model created unsustainable overtime due to loss of five part-time staff during transition.

- **Equipment & Technology Upgrade:** A $46,000 equipment line item increase (253% jump) covers server warranties, aging computer replacement, and implementation of RapidSOS software—a 911 enhancement providing real-time cell phone location data, automatic language translation (80-160 languages), and emergency contact information access. This addresses past reliance on state language line services that caused delays.

- **Mutual Aid Contribution:** The dispatch center receives a $10,000 annual stipend ($1,000 per full-time dispatcher) for serving as Essex County's fire control point. Some councilors questioned whether other communities should reimburse Beverly for this hub function, though this is a contractual arrangement from the 1970s.

- **Essex North Shore Agricultural & Technical School Budget:** Dr. Riccio presented a regional vocational school serving 1,927 students (223 from Beverly) across 58 communities and three counties. Budget increased 4.5% through enrollment growth and grant funding rather than operational cuts. The school implemented a straight lottery admissions system in February 2025 (per state mandate) and committed to capping Beverly in-district enrollment at 427 students for three years.

- **Staffing & Efficiency Concerns:** Multiple councilors pressed about potential budget cuts. Both dispatch and fire leadership emphasized that further reductions would harm response times and service quality. Chief O'Connor noted response times have improved significantly under the unified dispatch system and warned against cutting supervisor position or staffing levels.

- **Language Access & Community Data:** Dispatch center confirmed Spanish as primary language need, with recent addition of third Spanish-speaking dispatcher. Council member Sonya asked about tracking language call data to inform city-wide communication strategies; dispatch indicated state language line may have logs but nothing tracked in-house.

## Key Votes

No formal votes on budgets were taken during this portion of the meeting. This was a budget presentation and discussion session; votes are expected at a later council meeting.

---

### Notable Items

- **(Unclear from transcript):** Specific salary increase percentages for Essex Tech's recently ratified teacher contract were not disclosed, pending public release.
- Dispatch center offered to host council members for a facility tour to observe operations firsthand.
- Essex Tech reported 41% of incoming Beverly ninth-graders have learning disabilities, suggesting strong special education support.

title: Beverly City Council Budget Meeting 06/16/2026
headline: Beverly Council approves budget restructuring to preserve Veterans Services assistant position and maintains dental clinic funding through alternative sources.
bullets: **Veterans Services**: City redirected budget allocations to restore the assistant Veterans Services Officer (VSO) position to full-time status (0.44 FTE reduction avoided). The department manages over 1,000 active veterans' claims and brought $55.3 million in federal VA benefits to Beverly residents over 10 years. Memorial Day observance will scale back to standard ceremony (eliminating expanded "Warrior Weekend") to prioritize staff retention.

**Council on Aging**: Department absorbs roughly $90,000 in budget cuts while taking on CATA bus transportation duties (expected to save city $100,000+ annually). Daily senior attendance grew from 99 to 139 seniors since FY23; monthly transportation rides increased from 50 to 862. New shuttle service launching with fixed route (Mondays/Thursdays) and bi-weekly supermarket trips; exact route details still being finalized based on user surveys.

**Board of Health**: Preserved all staff positions by moving dental clinic funding (~$50,000-55,000/year) to alternative funding source, providing 4-5 year bridge before funds deplete. Dental clinic, unique statewide since 1918, serves vulnerable children with cleanings and fluoride treatments. Three environmental health inspectors maintained (matching peer cities like Peabody and Salem) to meet state-mandated inspection requirements.

**Budget Constraints**: All three departments facing significant reductions ($80,000+ each) amid structural budget pressures. Council members flagged FY28 as potentially much more difficult given limited municipal funding flexibility.

**Data Collection**: Departments committed to improving data sharing—Veterans Services will provide anonymized demographic data; Council on Aging exploring transportation usage metrics; Board of Health continuing compliance reporting.
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary

## Headline
Beverly Council approves budget restructuring to preserve Veterans Services assistant position and maintains dental clinic funding through alternative sources.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Veterans Services**: City redirected budget allocations to restore the assistant Veterans Services Officer (VSO) position to full-time status (0.44 FTE reduction avoided). The department manages over 1,000 active veterans' claims and brought $55.3 million in federal VA benefits to Beverly residents over 10 years. Memorial Day observance will scale back to standard ceremony (eliminating expanded "Warrior Weekend") to prioritize staff retention.

• **Council on Aging**: Department absorbs roughly $90,000 in budget cuts while taking on CATA bus transportation duties (expected to save city $100,000+ annually). Daily senior attendance grew from 99 to 139 seniors since FY23; monthly transportation rides increased from 50 to 862. New shuttle service launching with fixed route (Mondays/Thursdays) and bi-weekly supermarket trips; exact route details still being finalized based on user surveys.

• **Board of Health**: Preserved all staff positions by moving dental clinic funding (~$50,000-55,000/year) to alternative funding source, providing 4-5 year bridge before funds deplete. Dental clinic, unique statewide since 1918, serves vulnerable children with cleanings and fluoride treatments. Three environmental health inspectors maintained (matching peer cities like Peabody and Salem) to meet state-mandated inspection requirements.

• **Budget Constraints**: All three departments facing significant reductions ($80,000+ each) amid structural budget pressures. Council members flagged FY28 as potentially much more difficult given limited municipal funding flexibility.

• **Data Collection**: Departments committed to improving data sharing—Veterans Services will provide anonymized demographic data; Council on Aging exploring transportation usage metrics; Board of Health continuing compliance reporting.

## Key Votes

**(Unclear from transcript)** — No formal votes were recorded in this portion of the meeting. The presentation appeared to be informational/advisory regarding budget adjustments to be formalized in July.

---

**Note**: This was a Finance & Property Committee meeting with full Council present. Discussions focused on justifying service-critical positions and budget reallocation strategies rather than formal voting on specific articles.

title: Beverly City Council Meeting 06/15/2026
headline: City Council honors Bernard M. Gordon Foundation's $70,000 grant to Beverly High School robotics program and debates trash fee increases with new oversight requirements.
bullets: **Robotics Recognition**: The council unanimously approved a proclamation (Order 165) honoring the Bernard M. Gordon Foundation for $70,000 in grants to Beverly High School's robotics program. The foundation was impressed by recent graduate Mandy Harrington's grant-writing initiative and decided to invest in the school. Legendary inventor Bernard M. Gordon (age 99) created CT scanners, airport security technology, and other innovations.

**Trash Fee Amendment**: Council debated Order 142, which proposes residential trash fee increases from $150 to $300 per year (or $200 for smaller bins, $400 for larger). Councilor Retundo successfully amended the ordinance to require the city to review all trash fees by March 1st of every even-numbered year to prevent another 20+ year gap without review—though no fee increase is mandated by the review.

**Veteran Services Funding**: Five residents spoke passionately during public comment about preserving funding and full-time staffing for Beverly's Veterans Service Department, which serves 1,561 veterans with just two staff members. A dedicated subcommittee meeting on veteran services is scheduled for the next day (June 16) at 6 p.m. to address budget concerns.

**Multiple Grants Approved**: The council approved five grants totaling approximately $604,000, including: $100,000 for Beverly Police Department wellness programs, $156,000 for behavioral health training across Essex County, $248,478 for a jail diversion program, and $499,974 for a complete streets project on Cabot Street (delayed pending utility work).

**Deficit Reduction Committee**: Council approved appointments to a new special committee on deficit reduction, including school committee members and four citizen representatives, to address structural budget imbalances.
**Zoning Amendment**: Order 119 (accessory dwelling unit regulations required by state law) passed final vote unanimously.
votes: Accessory Dwelling Unit Zoning Amendment — ✅ Passed 9-0 (final passage)
Bernard M. Gordon Foundation Proclamation — ✅ Passed 9-0
Road Resurfacing Transfer ($1.5M) — ✅ Public hearing set for June 25, 6:30 p.m.
Lynch Park Climate Resiliency ($45K) — ✅ Public hearing set for June 25, 6:45 p.m.
Trash Fee Increases — (unclear from transcript) – Order tabled temporarily with amendment pending; full council vote not yet taken
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary
**June 15, 2026**

## Headline
City Council honors Bernard M. Gordon Foundation's $70,000 grant to Beverly High School robotics program and debates trash fee increases with new oversight requirements.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Robotics Recognition**: The council unanimously approved a proclamation (Order 165) honoring the Bernard M. Gordon Foundation for $70,000 in grants to Beverly High School's robotics program. The foundation was impressed by recent graduate Mandy Harrington's grant-writing initiative and decided to invest in the school. Legendary inventor Bernard M. Gordon (age 99) created CT scanners, airport security technology, and other innovations.

• **Trash Fee Amendment**: Council debated Order 142, which proposes residential trash fee increases from $150 to $300 per year (or $200 for smaller bins, $400 for larger). Councilor Retundo successfully amended the ordinance to require the city to review all trash fees by March 1st of every even-numbered year to prevent another 20+ year gap without review—though no fee increase is mandated by the review.

• **Veteran Services Funding**: Five residents spoke passionately during public comment about preserving funding and full-time staffing for Beverly's Veterans Service Department, which serves 1,561 veterans with just two staff members. A dedicated subcommittee meeting on veteran services is scheduled for the next day (June 16) at 6 p.m. to address budget concerns.

• **Multiple Grants Approved**: The council approved five grants totaling approximately $604,000, including: $100,000 for Beverly Police Department wellness programs, $156,000 for behavioral health training across Essex County, $248,478 for a jail diversion program, and $499,974 for a complete streets project on Cabot Street (delayed pending utility work).

• **Deficit Reduction Committee**: Council approved appointments to a new special committee on deficit reduction, including school committee members and four citizen representatives, to address structural budget imbalances.

• **Zoning Amendment**: Order 119 (accessory dwelling unit regulations required by state law) passed final vote unanimously.

## Key Votes

| Order | Item | Result |
|-------|------|--------|
| 119 | Accessory Dwelling Unit Zoning Amendment | ✅ Passed 9-0 (final passage) |
| 165 | Bernard M. Gordon Foundation Proclamation | ✅ Passed 9-0 |
| 166 | Road Resurfacing Transfer ($1.5M) | ✅ Public hearing set for June 25, 6:30 p.m. |
| 167 | Lynch Park Climate Resiliency ($45K) | ✅ Public hearing set for June 25, 6:45 p.m. |
| 168 | Police Wellness Grant ($100K) | ✅ Approved 3-0 (subcommittee) |
| 169 | Mental Health Training Grant ($156K) | ✅ Approved 3-0 (subcommittee) |
| 170 | Jail Diversion Program Grant ($248,478) | ✅ Approved 3-0 (subcommittee) |
| 171 | Veterans Dept. Donation ($2K) | ✅ Approved 3-0 (subcommittee) |
| 172 | Veterans Pastry Donation ($50) | ✅ Approved 3-0 (subcommittee) |
| 175 | Complete Streets Grant ($499,974) | ✅ Approved 3-0 (subcommittee) |
| 176 | Deficit Reduction Committee Appointments | ✅ Approved (subcommittee) |
| **142** | **Trash Fee Increases** | **(unclear from transcript)** – Order tabled temporarily with amendment pending; full council vote not yet taken |

---

**Note**: Order 142 (trash fees) was tabled during the main meeting to allow time for the solicitor to prepare written language for a new amendment requiring regular fee reviews. The council indicated intent to return to this vote later in the meeting.

title: Beverly City Council Budget Meeting 06/11/2026
headline: City Council schedules emergency Community Services budget hearing after scheduling oversight omitted library and veterans services from original agenda.
bullets: **Library Budget Presentation**: Library Director Allison Babin presented FY27 budget of ~$1.04M (1.3% of city budget), highlighting a $7M return on investment for residents. The budget includes service reductions: closing children's room early at main library (7pm Mon-Thu) and cutting Farms branch hours from 52 to 33 weekly hours, saving $80K but reducing a heavily-used community resource.

**Scheduling Correction**: Council discovered that Community Services (which includes library and veterans services) was not listed on the agenda, violating open meeting law. A new Community Services budget hearing was scheduled for Tuesday, January 16 at 6pm to address this oversight.

**Solicitor's Office Budget**: Beth Oldmixon presented the solicitor's budget at $540K (0.3% of budget), a $16K reduction. The office is reducing from 3 to 2.5 full-time attorneys but increasing outside counsel budget from $55K to $70K to handle upcoming collective bargaining negotiations and ordinance review.

**Police Department Budget**: Chief LaFleche presented an $11.2M budget (6.2% of budget) with only 1.2% increase. Key concerns: Department is 10+ officers below FBI recommended staffing levels (74 vs. 85-90 needed); no new cruisers allocated; overtime already exceeds $1.1M budget; proposed cuts include one sergeant and one patrol officer through attrition.

**Budget Deficit Discussion**: Council members debated whether cuts should target discretionary spending (consultants, travel, City Hall expansion) versus essential services (police, fire, library). Councilor St. Hilaire argued against cutting public safety and library services.
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary

## Headline
City Council schedules emergency Community Services budget hearing after scheduling oversight omitted library and veterans services from original agenda.

## Key Topics & Decisions

- **Library Budget Presentation**: Library Director Allison Babin presented FY27 budget of ~$1.04M (1.3% of city budget), highlighting a $7M return on investment for residents. The budget includes service reductions: closing children's room early at main library (7pm Mon-Thu) and cutting Farms branch hours from 52 to 33 weekly hours, saving $80K but reducing a heavily-used community resource.

- **Scheduling Correction**: Council discovered that Community Services (which includes library and veterans services) was not listed on the agenda, violating open meeting law. A new Community Services budget hearing was scheduled for Tuesday, January 16 at 6pm to address this oversight.

- **Solicitor's Office Budget**: Beth Oldmixon presented the solicitor's budget at $540K (0.3% of budget), a $16K reduction. The office is reducing from 3 to 2.5 full-time attorneys but increasing outside counsel budget from $55K to $70K to handle upcoming collective bargaining negotiations and ordinance review.

- **Police Department Budget**: Chief LaFleche presented an $11.2M budget (6.2% of budget) with only 1.2% increase. Key concerns: Department is 10+ officers below FBI recommended staffing levels (74 vs. 85-90 needed); no new cruisers allocated; overtime already exceeds $1.1M budget; proposed cuts include one sergeant and one patrol officer through attrition.

- **Budget Deficit Discussion**: Council members debated whether cuts should target discretionary spending (consultants, travel, City Hall expansion) versus essential services (police, fire, library). Councilor St. Hilaire argued against cutting public safety and library services.

## Key Votes

**(Unclear from transcript)** No formal votes were taken during this portion of the meeting. The council was in discussion/presentation mode for departmental budgets.

---

**Note**: Meeting remained in process when transcript ended; fire department, parks, public services, and other departments' budgets not yet discussed.

title: School Committee: CISL and Reg. School Committee Meeting 06/10/2026 (hybrid with captions)
headline: Emotional public comment on anti-Semitic incidents at Beverly Middle School and a push to restore ~$2M cut from the schools' budget dominated a meeting that also adopted new meeting norms and several housekeeping policies.
bullets: Anti-Semitism response: Multiple residents, including incoming Temple Beth Abraham president Julie Zeff, spoke about recent anti-Semitic graffiti and hate speech at Beverly Middle School, urging the district to respond with urgency. Superintendent Cushing described ongoing outreach to Jewish community leaders and said he is building a comprehensive response plan; he also noted a sharp rise in hate-related emails following the high school's pride flag raising.

Budget funding fight: Beverly Teachers Association member Andrea Sherman urged the committee and public to press the City Council to use its authority under Massachusetts law (Ch. 44 §32) to restore roughly $2 million the mayor cut from the school committee's previously approved "critical needs" budget, noting the city holds over $30M combined in free cash and stabilization reserves.

CISL subcommittee: All five elementary schools plus the middle and high school presented year-end School Improvement Plan updates covering literacy/discourse strategies, MTSS (multi-tiered student support) practices, and student belonging initiatives — informational, no votes. Year-end handbooks were advanced with only minor language updates ("caregiver" instead of "parent").

Process reform proposal: Beverly Teachers Association representative Casey Fiore proposed CISL subcommittee changes — advance distribution of presentation materials, time limits on presentations, and written monthly school updates — to give committee members more substantive insight into daily school operations.

Superintendent's entry findings: Dr. Cushing presented a first-year "entry findings" report assessing district strengths and opportunities across four budget-priority areas (learning opportunities, student supports, long-range planning, stakeholder communication), citing a structural funding deficit as an ongoing challenge.

New meeting norms: Adopted updated norms on electronic device use during meetings (a motion to soften the language failed 2-7) and approved three routine policies (student gifts/solicitations, caregiver/booster organization relations, school advertising) plus the FY27 meeting calendar.
votes: Amendment to soften electronic-device norms language — Failed 2-7
FY27 school committee meeting calendar — Approved 9-0
raw:
# Beverly School Committee Meeting Summary
**June 10, 2026 (CISL + Regular Meeting)**

## Headline
Emotional public comment on anti-Semitic incidents at Beverly Middle School and a push to restore ~$2M cut from the schools' budget dominated a meeting that also adopted new meeting norms and several housekeeping policies.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Anti-Semitism response** – Multiple residents spoke about anti-Semitic graffiti and hate speech at Beverly Middle School. The superintendent described ongoing outreach to Jewish community leaders and a forthcoming response plan.

• **Budget funding fight** – BTA member Andrea Sherman urged pressure on the City Council to restore ~$2M cut from the school committee's "critical needs" budget using state law authority.

• **CISL subcommittee** – All elementary, middle, and high schools presented year-end School Improvement Plan updates (informational). Handbooks advanced with minor language updates.

• **Process reform proposal** – BTA's CISL rep proposed advance materials, time limits, and written monthly updates to improve subcommittee effectiveness.

• **Superintendent's entry findings** – First-year assessment of district strengths/opportunities presented, citing structural budget deficit as ongoing challenge.

• **New meeting norms & policies** – Adopted electronic device use norms and three routine policies plus the FY27 calendar.

## Key Votes

| Item | Result |
|------|--------|
| Approve CISL minutes (4/29) | **Approved 11-0** |
| Accept warrants | **Approved 8-1** |
| Approve FY27 handbooks | **Approved 9-0** |
| Soften electronic-device norms (amendment) | **Failed 2-7** |
| Adopt meeting norms & protocols | **Approved** |
| Policy JP (gifts & solicitations) | **Approved 9-0** |
| Policy KBE (caregiver/booster orgs) | **Approved 9-0** |
| Policy KHB (advertising) | **Approved 9-0** |
| FY27 meeting calendar | **Approved 9-0** |

title: Beverly City Council Budget Meeting 06/09/2026
headline: City Council reviews FY2027 budgets for Planning and Fire departments amid significant staffing cuts and fiscal constraints.
bullets: **Planning Department Budget Cuts**: Director Darlene Wyn presented a $207,000 reduction (from $784K to $577K) due to loss of one full-time Associate Planner position through attrition. The department currently operates with only 4-5 technical staff compared to Salem's 13-15 planners, despite similar city sizes. Remaining staff will absorb duties including additional board/commission oversight and long-range planning projects.

**KADA Shuttle Service Discontinuation**: The Beverly Shuttle contract with KADA will end June 30th due to cost increases (anticipated 20-25% higher) and KADA's decision not to renew. Planning department is developing a community questionnaire to understand rider demographics and working with Mass DOT on alternative transportation solutions. Council expressed concerns about gaps for residents without cars and those over 60.

**Planning Department Consultant Budget Cut**: Consulting line reduced by 41% (~$5,000), limiting ability to hire external expertise for design standards and other projects. Department will attempt work in-house or use consultant raw files creatively.

**Fire Department Staffing Shift**: Chief O'Conor announced the full-time Training Officer position will be eliminated (first time since early 1990s), with that lieutenant shifting back to line duty while maintaining some training responsibilities. This affects the department's ability to manage emerging hazards like lithium-ion battery fires and electric vehicle incidents.

**Fire Department Overtime Concerns**: Combined General Emergency and Shift Coverage overtime lines show $53,000 reduction despite recent actuals exceeding projections. Chief believes this is sustainable but acknowledged it's not ideal; concerned that future budget cuts could force trucks out of service.

**Staffing Levels & Safety**: Fire Chief emphasized department operates below NFPA standards (currently 13 minimum per shift vs. NFPA recommendation of 17 on-scene within 8 minutes). Any further reductions risk forcing apparatus out of service, leaving sections of city without coverage.
raw:
# Beverly City Council Budget Meeting Summary

## Headline
City Council reviews FY2027 budgets for Planning and Fire departments amid significant staffing cuts and fiscal constraints.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Planning Department Budget Cuts**: Director Darlene Wyn presented a $207,000 reduction (from $784K to $577K) due to loss of one full-time Associate Planner position through attrition. The department currently operates with only 4-5 technical staff compared to Salem's 13-15 planners, despite similar city sizes. Remaining staff will absorb duties including additional board/commission oversight and long-range planning projects.

• **KADA Shuttle Service Discontinuation**: The Beverly Shuttle contract with KADA will end June 30th due to cost increases (anticipated 20-25% higher) and KADA's decision not to renew. Planning department is developing a community questionnaire to understand rider demographics and working with Mass DOT on alternative transportation solutions. Council expressed concerns about gaps for residents without cars and those over 60.

• **Planning Department Consultant Budget Cut**: Consulting line reduced by 41% (~$5,000), limiting ability to hire external expertise for design standards and other projects. Department will attempt work in-house or use consultant raw files creatively.

• **Fire Department Staffing Shift**: Chief O'Conor announced the full-time Training Officer position will be eliminated (first time since early 1990s), with that lieutenant shifting back to line duty while maintaining some training responsibilities. This affects the department's ability to manage emerging hazards like lithium-ion battery fires and electric vehicle incidents.

• **Fire Department Overtime Concerns**: Combined General Emergency and Shift Coverage overtime lines show $53,000 reduction despite recent actuals exceeding projections. Chief believes this is sustainable but acknowledged it's not ideal; concerned that future budget cuts could force trucks out of service.

• **Staffing Levels & Safety**: Fire Chief emphasized department operates below NFPA standards (currently 13 minimum per shift vs. NFPA recommendation of 17 on-scene within 8 minutes). Any further reductions risk forcing apparatus out of service, leaving sections of city without coverage.

## Key Votes

*No formal votes were recorded in this portion of the meeting transcript. This was a budget presentation and Q&A session; voting would occur at a later meeting.*

---

**Note**: The transcript ends mid-discussion. Final council votes on these budgets were not included in the provided material. (Unclear from transcript) whether the Planning and Fire budgets were ultimately approved as presented or modified.

title: Beverly City Council Meeting 05/18/2026
headline: After honoring a life-saving YMCA teacher and five exemplary youth, the council debated — but did not vote on — a proposed trash fee increase from $100 to as much as $425, amid a heated fight over whether the $30M City Hall renovation should be cut to offset the cost.
bullets: Trash fee debate (no final vote): The administration proposed raising the annual trash fee from $100 to $425 (for a 65-gallon cart) to cover a new $5.46M automated collection contract with Casella, up from $3.9M today. A subcommittee amendment lowered the proposal to $300/$210. Several councilors (St. Hilaire, Crowley, Mady) argued the increase was unaffordable and pushed to cancel or scale back the $30M City Hall renovation to offset costs instead; the mayor and others (Feldman, Houseman) countered that capital debt cannot legally be redirected to cover operating costs. The council postponed any vote and scheduled a special meeting for May 26th to continue the debate with public comment, ahead of the June 1st budget presentation.

CPA 13th round funding ($1.8M): After a public hearing, the council approved eight Community Preservation Act projects recommended by the CPC, including a $825,645 fire sprinkler system for Garden City Towers (a 137-resident senior housing complex flagged as a fire risk by the fire chief), $237,000 for Central Cemetery fence/gate repairs, three park/playground renovations (Gillis Park, Ahern/Goldway, Gilbert Park), and smaller grants for the Affordable Housing Trust and Garden School roof repair.

Recognitions: The council and mayor jointly proclaimed May 18 "Marie Paresi Day," honoring a YMCA preschool teacher who performed abdominal thrusts to save a choking toddler. Five students were also honored with the city's Exemplary Youth Awards for overcoming adversity.
Public comment: Two residents spoke on the trash fee — one opposing it as a sign of city financial mismanagement, another supporting it as a fairer alternative to further school funding cuts.

Routine business: Approved several donations (including an $8,015 library tech gift and fire department EMS supply donations), an Apple lease for student laptops, a taxi livery license renewal, two parking/traffic safety referrals in Beverly Farms and downtown, and set four public hearings for the June 1st meeting (road/sidewalk funding, OPEB transfer, snow & ice costs, and trash cart purchases).
votes: Order 142 (trash fee ordinance increase) — No final vote; motion withdrawn, postponed to May 26th special meeting
CPA: Central Cemetery Fence & Gates ($237,000) — Approved 8-0
CPA: Central Cemetery Tombhouse study ($23,390) — Approved 8-0 (Houseman recused)
CPA: Gillis Park Basketball Court ($130,155) — Approved 9-0
CPA: Gilbert Park Bartlett Gardens ($240,000) — Approved 9-0
CPA: Garden City Towers fire sprinklers ($825,645) — Approved 9-0
CPA: Affordable Housing Trust support (~$64,044) — Approved 9-0
CPA: Garden School roof replacement ($25,672) — Approved 9-0
Matthew Herot appointment, Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee — Approved 9-0
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary
**May 18, 2026**

## Headline
After honoring a life-saving YMCA teacher and five exemplary youth, the council debated — but did not vote on — a proposed trash fee increase from $100 to as much as $425, amid a heated fight over whether the $30M City Hall renovation should be cut to offset the cost.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Trash fee debate (no final vote)** – The administration proposed raising the annual trash fee from $100 to $425 (65-gallon cart) to cover a new $5.46M automated collection contract with Casella, up from $3.9M today. A subcommittee amendment lowered the proposal to $300/$210. Councilors St. Hilaire, Crowley, and Mady argued the increase was unaffordable and pushed to cancel or scale back the $30M City Hall renovation to offset costs; the mayor and Councilors Feldman and Houseman countered that capital debt cannot legally fund operating costs. The council postponed any vote, scheduling a special meeting for May 26th ahead of the June 1st budget presentation.

• **CPA 13th round funding ($1.8M)** – After a public hearing, approved eight Community Preservation Act projects, including $825,645 for fire sprinklers at Garden City Towers senior housing (flagged as a fire risk by the fire chief), $237,000 for Central Cemetery fence/gate repairs, three park renovations, and smaller grants for affordable housing and the Garden School roof.

• **Recognitions** – Proclaimed May 18 "Marie Paresi Day" for a YMCA teacher who saved a choking toddler's life, and honored five students with Exemplary Youth Awards.

• **Public comment** – Two residents spoke on the trash fee, one opposed and one in support as an alternative to school funding cuts.

• **Routine business** – Approved several donations, an Apple student laptop lease, a taxi license renewal, two traffic safety referrals, a board appointment, and set four public hearings for June 1st.

## Key Votes

| Order | Item | Result |
|-------|------|--------|
| 137 | Marie Paresi Day proclamation | **Approved 9-0** |
| 142 | Trash fee ordinance increase | **No vote — postponed to May 26** |
| SPA3 | CPA: Central Cemetery Fence & Gates ($237,000) | **Approved 8-0** |
| SPA2 | CPA: Central Cemetery Tombhouse study ($23,390) | **Approved 8-0** (Houseman recused) |
| R13-1 | CPA: Gillis Park Basketball Court ($130,155) | **Approved 9-0** |
| R13-2 | CPA: Ahern/Goldway Playground ($250,000) | **Approved 9-0** |
| R13-3 | CPA: Gilbert Park Bartlett Gardens ($240,000) | **Approved 9-0** |
| R13-4 | CPA: Garden City Towers fire sprinklers ($825,645) | **Approved 9-0** |
| R13-5 | CPA: Affordable Housing Trust support (~$64,044) | **Approved 9-0** |
| SPA4 | CPA: Garden School roof replacement ($25,672) | **Approved 9-0** |
| 132 | Matthew Herot appointment, Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee | **Approved 9-0** |

**Note:** The trash fee/City Hall debate dominated the meeting; no final vote was taken on Order 142.

title: Beverly City Council Special Meeting 04/29/2026
headline: In a brief remote special meeting, the council voted to set a June 1st public hearing on naming the road into Beverly Golf and Tennis in honor of a longtime community member.
bullets: Order 127 (Road naming): Filed by Councilor Rotondo, this order proposes naming the road leading into Beverly Golf and Tennis after a longtime community member (name unclear from transcript audio). The council voted to set a public hearing for the June 1st regular meeting at 7:30 PM, opening the required 30-day public comment period for naming city property.
Remote meeting format: The meeting was held entirely remotely, requiring roll-call votes for all council actions, including adjournment, rather than the usual voice vote.
The meeting was purely procedural — its sole purpose was to schedule the public hearing, not to vote on the naming itself.
votes: Set public hearing for Order 127 (road naming, June 1st 7:30 PM) — Approved 7-0
raw:
# Beverly City Council Meeting Summary
**April 29, 2026 (Special Meeting)**

## Headline
In a brief remote special meeting, the council voted to set a June 1st public hearing on naming the road into Beverly Golf and Tennis in honor of a longtime community member.

## Key Topics & Decisions

• **Order 127 (Road naming)** – Filed by Councilor Rotondo, this order proposes naming the road leading into Beverly Golf and Tennis after a longtime community member (name unclear from transcript audio). The council voted to set a public hearing for the June 1st regular meeting at 7:30 PM, opening the required 30-day public comment period for naming city property.

• **Remote meeting format** – The meeting was held entirely remotely, requiring roll-call votes for all council actions, including adjournment, rather than the usual voice vote.

• This was a short, purely procedural meeting — its sole purpose was to schedule the public hearing, not to vote on the naming itself.

## Key Votes

| Order | Item | Result |
|-------|------|--------|
| 127 | Set public hearing on road naming (June 1, 7:30 PM) | **Approved 7-0** |
| — | Adjournment | **Approved 7-0** |
